[[De:Tanigawa Ken'ichi‎]] '''Tanigawa Ken'ichi''' (Japanese: 谷川 健一; born July 28, 1921, in Minamata; died August 24, 2013) was one of Japan's most prominent cultural anthropologists, particularly known for his work on historical toponymy (the study of place names).
==Biography==
After attending middle[[Category:middle]] school in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).

{{画像提供依頼|顔写真|date=2021年1月|cat=人物}} {{Infobox 学者 | name = 谷川 健一 | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1921|7|28}} | birth_place = {{JPN}}[[熊本県]][[水俣市|水俣]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2013|8|24|1921|7|28}} | death_place = | othername = |main_interests = [[民俗学]]、[[地名学]]、[[日本文学]] |alma_mater = [[東京大学]] |workplaces = [[近畿大学]] |degree = <!--学位--> | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children =[[谷川章雄]](考古学者) }} '''谷川 健一'''（たにがわ けんいち、[[1921年]][[7月28日]] - [[2013年]][[8月24日]]<ref name="nikkei">{{Cite news|url= https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASDG2402Z_U3A820C1CC1000/ |title= 谷川健一氏が死去 民俗学者・文化功労者 |newspaper= 日本経済新聞 |publisher= 日本経済新聞社 |date= 2013-08-25 |accessdate= 2020-03-22 }}</ref>）は、[[日本]]の[[民俗学者]]・[[地名学]]者・[[作家]]・[[歌人]]。[[近畿大学]]教授・[[日本地名研究所]]所長等を歴任。2007年[[文化功労者]]選出。
在野の[[研究者|学者]]として[[日本文学]]や[[民俗学]]の研究をおこない多くの研究書を著した。日本文学の源流を[[沖縄県|沖縄]]・[[鹿児島県|鹿児島]]などの謡にもとめた「南島文学発生論」などの業績をあげた。
==経歴== [[File:Chunichi1967-03-11-1.jpg|thumb|240px|『太陽』1967年4月号の新聞広告]] ;出生から修学期 1921年、[[熊本県]][[水俣市|水俣]]で六人兄弟の長男として生まれた。[[熊本県立熊本高等学校|熊本中学]]、[[浪速高等学校 (旧制)]]を経て、[[東京大学|東京帝国大学]][[東京大学大学院人文社会系研究科・文学部|文学部]]に進学。しかし東京大学入学後、[[結核]]を患い、各地の療養所を転々とした<ref name="yomiuri">読売人物データベース</ref>。大学では[[フランス文学]]専攻で学び、卒業。
;平凡社勤務時代 30歳を過ぎた頃より[[編集者]]として[[平凡社]]に勤務<ref name="yomiuri"/>、『風土記日本』（1957-60年）、『日本残酷物語』（1959-61年）などを企画・編集し、1963年創刊の『[[太陽 (平凡社)|太陽]]』初代編集長を務めた。しかしながら結核が再発したことにより療養生活に入り、40代で退社<ref name="yomiuri"/>。
;作家として その後は執筆活動に重点を置き、1966年、初の著作で[[歴史小説]]『最後の攘夷党』が第55回[[直木三十五賞|直木賞]]候補になった。[[1970年代]]には『青銅の神の足跡』や『鍛冶屋の母』などを発表。民俗事象と文献資料に独自の分析を加え、[[柳田國男]]や[[折口信夫]]らの学問を批判的に展開し、多くの柳田・折口論考を記した。
1978年、「地名を守る会」を結成<ref name="yomiuri"/>。1981年、[[神奈川県]][[川崎市]]に「日本地名研究所」を設立し、所長に就いた。長い歴史を経てきた地名が[[町村合併]]などに伴って安易に地名変更されるという状況に警鐘を鳴らすなどした。2008年、日本地名研究所は[[神奈川文化賞]]を受賞した。
1987年から1996年まで、[[近畿大学]]教授・同大学民俗学研究所長を務めた。1994年、宮古島の森と信仰行事を保存する目的で「[[宮古島]]の神と森を考える会」が発足し、会長を務めた<ref name="yomiuri"/><ref>[[沖縄県]][[宮古島市]]に本部を置き、事務局長は佐渡山安公。2014年、谷川健一没後も活動を継続している。</ref>。1999年、文化交流誌『青』を創刊し、主宰した<ref name="yomiuri"/>。2001年には同人誌『花礁』を創刊し、主宰した<ref name="yomiuri"/>。
2009年1月、[[歌会始]]に[[召人]]として『陽に染まる飛魚の羽きらきらし海中（わたなか）に春の潮（うしほ）生れて』が朗詠された。
2013年に死去（[[満年齢|満]]92歳没）。
== 受賞・栄典 == *1973年：共編『日本庶民生活史料集成』(全20巻)で[[毎日出版文化賞]] *1986年：共編『日本民俗文化大系』(全14巻)で毎日出版文化賞特別賞 *1991年：『南島文学発生論』で[[芸術選奨]]文部大臣賞 *1992年：[[南方熊楠賞]]を受賞<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=第1回～第10回南方熊楠賞受賞者|website=南方熊楠顕彰館|url=https://www.minakata.org/kumagusuminakataprize/history/1st_10th/ |accessdate=2022-08-16}}</ref>。 *1992年：川崎市文化賞 *2001年：『海霊・水の女』で[[短歌研究賞]] *2007年：秋に[[文化功労者]]に選ばれた。 *[[2008年]] - [[神奈川文化賞]]
==研究内容・業績== 柳田が提唱した「日本民俗学」がのち組織化が進み善かれ悪しかれ学術化、専門科学化していったのに対し、谷川は、その職業的な背景もあり、在野精神を貫き学会と距離をとった独自の執筆活動を進めた<ref>著書『独学のすすめ』参照。ちなみに[[梅田望夫]]は自身のブログで、この本に勇気づけられたと回想した。</ref>。
折口を彷彿とさせる小説家的想像力と、柳田の影響を受けた民俗資料の編纂とがない交ぜになっており、その業績は各界から注目された。従来にない新たな日本像を加えるべく『海と列島文化』（全10巻別巻、[[小学館]]、1990-93年）を、日本史の[[網野善彦]]、[[文化人類学]]の[[大林太良]]、民俗学の[[宮田登]]、[[考古学]]の[[森浩一]]と共同編集<ref>〈海と列島文化〉完結記念に伴い、シンポジウム（討論記録）『日本像を問い直す』（小学館、1993年）を出版。</ref>している。 著名作は上記の他に『日本の地名』(正続)、『常世論』、『魔の系譜』、『神・人間・動物』など、小説作品も著し、1988年に小説『海の群星』は、[[緒形拳]]、[[石田ゆり子]]、[[織本順吉]]など出演で、[[日本放送協会|NHK]]ドラマ化された。
1980年代に『谷川健一著作集』（全10巻）が、2007年から2013年（没する直前に完結）に『谷川健一著全集』（全24巻）が刊行されており、その著作はまとめられている。
==家族・親族== *父：眼科の開業医<ref>谷川健一『谷川健一 : 越境する民俗学の巨人 : 追悼総特集』河出書房新社、205ページ、2014年、ISBN 978-4-309-74052-2</ref>。。 *弟：[[谷川雁]](谷川巌)は詩人。 *弟：[[谷川道雄]]は東洋史家。 *弟：[[吉田公彦]]（旧名 谷川公彦）は：[[日本エディタースクール]]創設者。 *長男：[[谷川章雄]]は考古学者。[[早稲田大学]]教授。 なお、日本地名研究所をひきついだ[[谷川彰英]]とは血縁関係はない。
== 著作 == ===著書=== {{colbegin|2}} *『最後の攘夷党』[[三一新書]] 1966 *『沖縄：辺境の時間と空間』三一書房 1970 *『魔の系譜』[[紀伊国屋書店]] 1971 **文庫化 [[講談社学術文庫]] 1984 *『常民への照射』[[冬樹社]] 1971 *『孤島文化論』[[潮出版社]] 1972 *『埋もれた日本地図』[[筑摩書房]] 1972 **文庫化 講談社学術文庫 2021 *『原風土の相貌』[[大和書房]] 1974 *『民俗の神』[[淡交社]] 1975 *『古代史ノオト』大和書房 1975 *『神・人間・動物：伝承を生きる世界』[[平凡社]] 1975 **文庫化 講談社学術文庫 1986 *『私説[[神風連]]』[[新人物往来社]] 1975 *『女の風土記』[[読売新聞社]] 1975 **文庫化 講談社学術文庫 1985 *『[[黒潮]]の民俗学：神々のいる風景』筑摩書房 1976 *『青と白の幻想』三一書房 1979 *『[[青銅]]の神の足跡』[[集英社]] 1979 **文庫化 [[集英社文庫]] 1989 **新書化 [[小学館ライブラリー]] 1995 *『[[鍛冶屋]]の母』思索社 1979 **文庫化 講談社学術文庫 1985 *『北国からの旅人：沖縄との出会い』筑摩書房 1980 *『神は細部に宿り給う：地名と[[民俗学]]』[[人文書院]] 1980 *『海の群星』集英社 1981 **文庫化 集英社文庫 1987 *『わたしの「天地始之事」』筑摩書房 1982 *『常世論 日本人の魂のゆくえ』[[平凡社選書]] 1983 **文庫化 講談社学術文庫 1989 *『失われた日本を求めて』[[青土社]] 1983 *『[[ヤマトタケル|白鳥伝説]]』集英社 1986 **文庫化 集英社文庫 1988 **新書化 小学館ライブラリー 1997 *『南島論序説』講談社学術文庫 1987<ref>本書は再編本。</ref> *『私の民俗学』[[東海大学出版会]] 1987 *『民俗・地名そして日本』[[同成社]] 1989 *『海の夫人』[[河出書房新社]] 1989 *『[[大嘗祭]]の成立 民俗文化論からの展開』[[小学館]] 1990 *『南島文学発生論』[[思潮社]] 1991 *『民俗の宇宙』三一書房 1993 *『海神の贈物：民俗の思想』小学館 1994 *『民俗の思想：常民の世界観と[[死生観]]』岩波書店(同時代ライブラリー) 1996<ref>本書は再編本。</ref> *『古代海人の世界』小学館 1995 *『独学のすすめ：時代を超えた巨人たち』[[晶文社]] 1996 *『沖縄：その危機と神々』講談社学術文庫 1996<ref>本書は再編本。</ref> *『日本の地名』[[岩波新書]] 1997 *『続・日本の地名』岩波新書 1998 *『日本の神々』岩波新書 1999 *『うたと日本人』[[講談社現代新書]] 2000 *『神に追われて』[[新潮社]] 2000 **文庫化 河出文庫 2022 *『[[柳田國男|柳田国男]]の民俗学』岩波新書 2001 *『古代人のコスモロジー：史話日本の古代 別巻』[[作品社]] 2003 *『心にひびく小さき民のことば』[[岩波書店]] 2004 *『渚の思想』晶文社 2004 *『[[四天王寺]]の鷹：謎の[[秦氏]]と[[物部氏]]を追って』[[河出書房新社]] 2006 **文庫化 河出文庫 2021 *『水俣再生への道』[[熊本日日新聞]]社 2006 *『甦る海上の道・日本と琉球』[[文春新書]] 2007 *『[[明治時代|明治]][[三文オペラ]]：巫娼から[[遊女]]へ』[[現代書館]] 2007 *『隠された物部王国「日本（ヒノモト）」』[[情報センター出版局]] 2008 *『妣の国への旅：[[私の履歴書]]』[[日本経済新聞]]出版社 2009 *『賎民の異神と芸能：山人・浮浪人・非人』河出書房新社 2009 {{colend}} ;以下は再編本 *『古代学への招待』日経ビジネス人文庫 2010 *『列島縦断地名逍遥』[[冨山房インターナショナル]] 2010 *『蛇：不死と再生の民俗』冨山房インターナショナル 2012 *『日本人の魂のゆくえ 古代日本と琉球の死生観』冨山房インターナショナル, 2012 *『魂の還る処 常世考』やまかわうみ別冊・アーツアンドクラフツ, 2013 *『柳田民俗学存疑：稲作一元論批判』[[冨山房インターナショナル]], 2014 *『谷川健一：民俗のこころと思想』[[前田速夫]]編、アーツアンドクラフツ(やまかわうみ別冊) 2016
=== 著作集 === *'''『谷川健一著作集』'''(全10巻) [[三一書房]] 1980-1988 {{colbegin|2}} #民俗学篇1「魔の系譜／神・人間・動物」 #民俗学篇2「民俗の神／民俗紀行」 #民俗学篇3「柳田学と折口学」 #古代学篇1「古代史ノオト／信仰と神話」 #古代学篇2「青銅の神の足跡／鍛冶屋の母」 #沖縄学篇「琉球弧の世界」 #女性史篇「女の風土記／無告の民」 #民俗学篇4「常世論／日本人の宇宙観／国津神と天津神」 #民俗学篇5「地名と文化／風土と民俗／小さき神」 #文学篇「海の群星／わたしの「天地始之事」／最後の攘夷党」 {{colend|2}}
*'''『谷川健一全集』'''(全24巻) [[冨山房#関連会社|冨山房インターナショナル]] 2006-2013 {{colbegin|2}} #古代一 白鳥伝説 #古代二 大嘗祭の成立／日本の神々 #古代三 古代史ノオト ほか #古代四 神・人間・動物／古代海人の世界 #沖縄一 南島文学発生論 #沖縄二 沖縄・辺境の時間と空間 #沖縄三 甦る海上の道・日本と琉球／渚の思想 #沖縄四 海の群星／神に追われて #民俗一 青銅の神の足跡／鍛冶屋の母 #民俗二 女の風土記／埋もれた日本地図(抄録) #民俗三 わたしの民俗学 #民俗四 魔の系譜／常世論 #民俗五 民間信仰史研究序説 #地名一 日本の地名 続日本の地名 #地名二 地名伝承を求めて／日本地名研究所の歩み #地名三 列島縦断地名逍遥 #短歌 谷川健一全歌集 #人物一 柳田国男 #人物二 独学のすすめ #創作 最後の攘夷党／私説[[神風連]]／明治[[三文オペラ]] #古代・人物補遺 四天王寺の鷹 人物論 #評論一 常民への照射（抄録）／評論 講演 #評論二 失われた日本を求めて(抄録)／評論／随想 #総索引 年譜／収録作品一覧 {{colend|2}} :最終24巻目は、総索引、人名・神名、地名、寺社名、聖地・聖域、書名、事項、年譜、収録作品一覧
*'''『谷川健一コレクション』'''(全6巻) 冨山房インターナショナル 2019-2020 :本書は全集未収録の著作を集成したもの {{colbegin|2}} #『小さきものへ』 #『わが沖縄』 #『日本の原像：民俗と古代』 #『日本の神と文化』 #『地名の世界』 #『孤高と誇り』 {{colend|2}}

==主宰誌== *『青』：1999年創刊。[[根釧原野]]から[[八重山]]の礁湖にいたる日本列島を縦断する文化交流誌。2005年に第7号を刊行して廃刊。 *『花礁』(はなぜ)：2001年創刊。南九州と南西諸島をフィールドとする同人誌。
== 谷川健一に関する書籍 == *『「青」の民俗学：谷川健一の世界』[[岡谷公二]]・[[山下欣一]]編、三一書房 1997 *『魂の民俗学：谷川健一の思想』大江修編、[[冨山房インターナショナル]] 2006 *『谷川健一：越境する民俗学の巨人 追悼総特集』 [[河出書房新社]]([[KAWADE道の手帖]]) 2014 *『谷川健一の世界：魂の民俗学が遺したもの』大江修編、冨山房インターナショナル 2016 *『地名と風土』(人間と大地をむすぶ情報誌 15) 日本地名研究所編・出版 2022<ref>生誕百年記念。</ref> #特集1 谷川健一論の現在 #特集2 谷川健一を語る *『谷川健一と谷川雁：精神の空洞化に抗して』[[前田速夫]]編、冨山房インターナショナル 2022

{{画像提供依頼|顔写真|date=2021年1月|cat=人物}} {{Infobox 学者 | name = 谷川 健一 | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1921|7|28}} | birth_place = {{JPN}}[[熊本県]][[水俣市|水俣]] | death_date = {{death date and age|2013|8|24|1921|7|28}} | death_place = | othername = |main_interests = [[民俗学]]、[[地名学]]、[[日本文学]] |alma_mater = [[東京大学]] |workplaces = [[近畿大学]] |degree = <!--学位--> | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children =[[谷川章雄]](考古学者) }} '''谷川 健一'''（たにがわ けんいち、[[1921年]][[7月28日]] - [[2013年]][[8月24日]]<ref name="nikkei">{{Cite news|url= https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXNASDG2402Z_U3A820C1CC1000/ |title= 谷川健一氏が死去 民俗学者・文化功労者 |newspaper= 日本経済新聞 |publisher= 日本経済新聞社 |date= 2013-08-25 |accessdate= 2020-03-22 }}</ref>）は、[[日本]]の[[民俗学者]]・[[地名学]]者・[[作家]]・[[歌人]]。[[近畿大学]]教授・[[日本地名研究所]]所長等を歴任。2007年[[文化功労者]]選出。
在野の[[研究者|学者]]として[[日本文学]]や[[民俗学]]の研究をおこない多くの研究書を著した。日本文学の源流を[[沖縄県|沖縄]]・[[鹿児島県|鹿児島]]などの謡にもとめた「南島文学発生論」などの業績をあげた。
==経歴== [[File:Chunichi1967-03-11-1.jpg|thumb|240px|『太陽』1967年4月号の新聞広告]] ;出生から修学期 1921年、[[熊本県]][[水俣市|水俣]]で六人兄弟の長男として生まれた。[[熊本県立熊本高等学校|熊本中学]]、[[浪速高等学校 (旧制)]]を経て、[[東京大学|東京帝国大学]][[東京大学大学院人文社会系研究科・文学部|文学部]]に進学。しかし東京大学入学後、[[結核]]を患い、各地の療養所を転々とした<ref name="yomiuri">読売人物データベース</ref>。大学では[[フランス文学]]専攻で学び、卒業。
;平凡社勤務時代 30歳を過ぎた頃より[[編集者]]として[[平凡社]]に勤務<ref name="yomiuri"/>、『風土記日本』（1957-60年）、『日本残酷物語』（1959-61年）などを企画・編集し、1963年創刊の『[[太陽 (平凡社)|太陽]]』初代編集長を務めた。しかしながら結核が再発したことにより療養生活に入り、40代で退社<ref name="yomiuri"/>。
;作家として その後は執筆活動に重点を置き、1966年、初の著作で[[歴史小説]]『最後の攘夷党』が第55回[[直木三十五賞|直木賞]]候補になった。[[1970年代]]には『青銅の神の足跡』や『鍛冶屋の母』などを発表。民俗事象と文献資料に独自の分析を加え、[[柳田國男]]や[[折口信夫]]らの学問を批判的に展開し、多くの柳田・折口論考を記した。
1978年、「地名を守る会」を結成<ref name="yomiuri"/>。1981年、[[神奈川県]][[川崎市]]に「日本地名研究所」を設立し、所長に就いた。長い歴史を経てきた地名が[[町村合併]]などに伴って安易に地名変更されるという状況に警鐘を鳴らすなどした。2008年、日本地名研究所は[[神奈川文化賞]]を受賞した。
1987年から1996年まで、[[近畿大学]]教授・同大学民俗学研究所長を務めた。1994年、宮古島の森と信仰行事を保存する目的で「[[宮古島]]の神と森を考える会」が発足し、会長を務めた<ref name="yomiuri"/><ref>[[沖縄県]][[宮古島市]]に本部を置き、事務局長は佐渡山安公。2014年、谷川健一没後も活動を継続している。</ref>。1999年、文化交流誌『青』を創刊し、主宰した<ref name="yomiuri"/>。2001年には同人誌『花礁』を創刊し、主宰した<ref name="yomiuri"/>。
2009年1月、[[歌会始]]に[[召人]]として『陽に染まる飛魚の羽きらきらし海中（わたなか）に春の潮（うしほ）生れて』が朗詠された。
2013年に死去（[[満年齢|満]]92歳没）。
== 受賞・栄典 == *1973年：共編『日本庶民生活史料集成』(全20巻)で[[毎日出版文化賞]] *1986年：共編『日本民俗文化大系』(全14巻)で毎日出版文化賞特別賞 *1991年：『南島文学発生論』で[[芸術選奨]]文部大臣賞 *1992年：[[南方熊楠賞]]を受賞<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=第1回～第10回南方熊楠賞受賞者|website=南方熊楠顕彰館|url=https://www.minakata.org/kumagusuminakataprize/history/1st_10th/ |accessdate=2022-08-16}}</ref>。 *1992年：川崎市文化賞 *2001年：『海霊・水の女』で[[短歌研究賞]] *2007年：秋に[[文化功労者]]に選ばれた。 *[[2008年]] - [[神奈川文化賞]]
==研究内容・業績== 柳田が提唱した「日本民俗学」がのち組織化が進み善かれ悪しかれ学術化、専門科学化していったのに対し、谷川は、その職業的な背景もあり、在野精神を貫き学会と距離をとった独自の執筆活動を進めた<ref>著書『独学のすすめ』参照。ちなみに[[梅田望夫]]は自身のブログで、この本に勇気づけられたと回想した。</ref>。
折口を彷彿とさせる小説家的想像力と、柳田の影響を受けた民俗資料の編纂とがない交ぜになっており、その業績は各界から注目された。従来にない新たな日本像を加えるべく『海と列島文化』（全10巻別巻、[[小学館]]、1990-93年）を、日本史の[[網野善彦]]、[[文化人類学]]の[[大林太良]]、民俗学の[[宮田登]]、[[考古学]]の[[森浩一]]と共同編集<ref>〈海と列島文化〉完結記念に伴い、シンポジウム（討論記録）『日本像を問い直す』（小学館、1993年）を出版。</ref>している。 著名作は上記の他に『日本の地名』(正続)、『常世論』、『魔の系譜』、『神・人間・動物』など、小説作品も著し、1988年に小説『海の群星』は、[[緒形拳]]、[[石田ゆり子]]、[[織本順吉]]など出演で、[[日本放送協会|NHK]]ドラマ化された。
1980年代に『谷川健一著作集』（全10巻）が、2007年から2013年（没する直前に完結）に『谷川健一著全集』（全24巻）が刊行されており、その著作はまとめられている。
==家族・親族== *父：眼科の開業医<ref>谷川健一『谷川健一 : 越境する民俗学の巨人 : 追悼総特集』河出書房新社、205ページ、2014年、ISBN 978-4-309-74052-2</ref>。。 *弟：[[谷川雁]](谷川巌)は詩人。 *弟：[[谷川道雄]]は東洋史家。 *弟：[[吉田公彦]]（旧名 谷川公彦）は：[[日本エディタースクール]]創設者。 *長男：[[谷川章雄]]は考古学者。[[早稲田大学]]教授。 なお、日本地名研究所をひきついだ[[谷川彰英]]とは血縁関係はない。
== 著作 == ===著書=== {{colbegin|2}} *『最後の攘夷党』[[三一新書]] 1966 *『沖縄：辺境の時間と空間』三一書房 1970 *『魔の系譜』[[紀伊国屋書店]] 1971 **文庫化 [[講談社学術文庫]] 1984 *『常民への照射』[[冬樹社]] 1971 *『孤島文化論』[[潮出版社]] 1972 *『埋もれた日本地図』[[筑摩書房]] 1972 **文庫化 講談社学術文庫 2021 *『原風土の相貌』[[大和書房]] 1974 *『民俗の神』[[淡交社]] 1975 *『古代史ノオト』大和書房 1975 *『神・人間・動物：伝承を生きる世界』[[平凡社]] 1975 **文庫化 講談社学術文庫 1986 *『私説[[神風連]]』[[新人物往来社]] 1975 *『女の風土記』[[読売新聞社]] 1975 **文庫化 講談社学術文庫 1985 *『[[黒潮]]の民俗学：神々のいる風景』筑摩書房 1976 *『青と白の幻想』三一書房 1979 *『[[青銅]]の神の足跡』[[集英社]] 1979 **文庫化 [[集英社文庫]] 1989 **新書化 [[小学館ライブラリー]] 1995 *『[[鍛冶屋]]の母』思索社 1979 **文庫化 講談社学術文庫 1985 *『北国からの旅人：沖縄との出会い』筑摩書房 1980 *『神は細部に宿り給う：地名と[[民俗学]]』[[人文書院]] 1980 *『海の群星』集英社 1981 **文庫化 集英社文庫 1987 *『わたしの「天地始之事」』筑摩書房 1982 *『常世論 日本人の魂のゆくえ』[[平凡社選書]] 1983 **文庫化 講談社学術文庫 1989 *『失われた日本を求めて』[[青土社]] 1983 *『[[ヤマトタケル|白鳥伝説]]』集英社 1986 **文庫化 集英社文庫 1988 **新書化 小学館ライブラリー 1997 *『南島論序説』講談社学術文庫 1987<ref>本書は再編本。</ref> *『私の民俗学』[[東海大学出版会]] 1987 *『民俗・地名そして日本』[[同成社]] 1989 *『海の夫人』[[河出書房新社]] 1989 *『[[大嘗祭]]の成立 民俗文化論からの展開』[[小学館]] 1990 *『南島文学発生論』[[思潮社]] 1991 *『民俗の宇宙』三一書房 1993 *『海神の贈物：民俗の思想』小学館 1994 *『民俗の思想：常民の世界観と[[死生観]]』岩波書店(同時代ライブラリー) 1996<ref>本書は再編本。</ref> *『古代海人の世界』小学館 1995 *『独学のすすめ：時代を超えた巨人たち』[[晶文社]] 1996 *『沖縄：その危機と神々』講談社学術文庫 1996<ref>本書は再編本。</ref> *『日本の地名』[[岩波新書]] 1997 *『続・日本の地名』岩波新書 1998 *『日本の神々』岩波新書 1999 *『うたと日本人』[[講談社現代新書]] 2000 *『神に追われて』[[新潮社]] 2000 **文庫化 河出文庫 2022 *『[[柳田國男|柳田国男]]の民俗学』岩波新書 2001 *『古代人のコスモロジー：史話日本の古代 別巻』[[作品社]] 2003 *『心にひびく小さき民のことば』[[岩波書店]] 2004 *『渚の思想』晶文社 2004 *『[[四天王寺]]の鷹：謎の[[秦氏]]と[[物部氏]]を追って』[[河出書房新社]] 2006 **文庫化 河出文庫 2021 *『水俣再生への道』[[熊本日日新聞]]社 2006 *『甦る海上の道・日本と琉球』[[文春新書]] 2007 *『[[明治時代|明治]][[三文オペラ]]：巫娼から[[遊女]]へ』[[現代書館]] 2007 *『隠された物部王国「日本（ヒノモト）」』[[情報センター出版局]] 2008 *『妣の国への旅：[[私の履歴書]]』[[日本経済新聞]]出版社 2009 *『賎民の異神と芸能：山人・浮浪人・非人』河出書房新社 2009 {{colend}} ;以下は再編本 *『古代学への招待』日経ビジネス人文庫 2010 *『列島縦断地名逍遥』[[冨山房インターナショナル]] 2010 *『蛇：不死と再生の民俗』冨山房インターナショナル 2012 *『日本人の魂のゆくえ 古代日本と琉球の死生観』冨山房インターナショナル, 2012 *『魂の還る処 常世考』やまかわうみ別冊・アーツアンドクラフツ, 2013 *『柳田民俗学存疑：稲作一元論批判』[[冨山房インターナショナル]], 2014 *『谷川健一：民俗のこころと思想』[[前田速夫]]編、アーツアンドクラフツ(やまかわうみ別冊) 2016
=== 著作集 === *'''『谷川健一著作集』'''(全10巻) [[三一書房]] 1980-1988 {{colbegin|2}} #民俗学篇1「魔の系譜／神・人間・動物」 #民俗学篇2「民俗の神／民俗紀行」 #民俗学篇3「柳田学と折口学」 #古代学篇1「古代史ノオト／信仰と神話」 #古代学篇2「青銅の神の足跡／鍛冶屋の母」 #沖縄学篇「琉球弧の世界」 #女性史篇「女の風土記／無告の民」 #民俗学篇4「常世論／日本人の宇宙観／国津神と天津神」 #民俗学篇5「地名と文化／風土と民俗／小さき神」 #文学篇「海の群星／わたしの「天地始之事」／最後の攘夷党」 {{colend|2}}
*'''『谷川健一全集』'''(全24巻) [[冨山房#関連会社|冨山房インターナショナル]] 2006-2013 {{colbegin|2}} #古代一 白鳥伝説 #古代二 大嘗祭の成立／日本の神々 #古代三 古代史ノオト ほか #古代四 神・人間・動物／古代海人の世界 #沖縄一 南島文学発生論 #沖縄二 沖縄・辺境の時間と空間 #沖縄三 甦る海上の道・日本と琉球／渚の思想 #沖縄四 海の群星／神に追われて #民俗一 青銅の神の足跡／鍛冶屋の母 #民俗二 女の風土記／埋もれた日本地図(抄録) #民俗三 わたしの民俗学 #民俗四 魔の系譜／常世論 #民俗五 民間信仰史研究序説 #地名一 日本の地名 続日本の地名 #地名二 地名伝承を求めて／日本地名研究所の歩み #地名三 列島縦断地名逍遥 #短歌 谷川健一全歌集 #人物一 柳田国男 #人物二 独学のすすめ #創作 最後の攘夷党／私説[[神風連]]／明治[[三文オペラ]] #古代・人物補遺 四天王寺の鷹 人物論 #評論一 常民への照射（抄録）／評論 講演 #評論二 失われた日本を求めて(抄録)／評論／随想 #総索引 年譜／収録作品一覧 {{colend|2}} :最終24巻目は、総索引、人名・神名、地名、寺社名、聖地・聖域、書名、事項、年譜、収録作品一覧
*'''『谷川健一コレクション』'''(全6巻) 冨山房インターナショナル 2019-2020 :本書は全集未収録の著作を集成したもの {{colbegin|2}} #『小さきものへ』 #『わが沖縄』 #『日本の原像：民俗と古代』 #『日本の神と文化』 #『地名の世界』 #『孤高と誇り』 {{colend|2}}
=== 対談集 === *『民俗論の原像：谷川健一対談集』伝統と現代社 1974 *『民俗学の遠近法：谷川健一対談集』[[東海大学出版会]] 1981 *『対談集 地名と風土』思潮社 1991 *『源泉の思考：谷川健一対談集』冨山房インターナショナル 2008
===編著・共著 === {{colbegin|2}} *『女性残酷物語』(全2巻) [[大和書房]] 1968 *『叢書わが沖縄』(全6巻) 木耳社 1970-1971 *『[[娼婦]]』(近代民衆の記録 3) [[新人物往来社]] 1971 *『沖縄の証言』名嘉正八郎共編、[[中公新書]] 1971 *『[[アイヌ]]』(近代民衆の記録 5) 新人物往来社 1972 *『人と思想：[[折口信夫]]』[[三一書房]] 1974 *『日本文化の源流を求めて』[[金達寿]]共編、[[筑摩書房]] 1975 *『日本古代文化の原像』[[大林太良]]共編、三一書房 1977 *『出雲の神々』[[石元泰博]]写真、平凡社カラー新書 1978 **新版 1997年 *『現代「地名」考』編著 [[日本放送出版協会]]([[NHKブックス]]) 1979 *『地名の話』平凡社選書 1979 *『神々の島：沖縄[[久高島]]のまつり』比嘉康雄共著、[[平凡社]] 1979 *『柳田国男と折口信夫』[[池田弥三郎]]と対話、思索社 1980 **再版 岩波書店同時代ライブラリー 1994 *『産屋の民俗』西山やよい共著 [[国書刊行会]], 1981 *『地名と風土：日本人と大地を結ぶ シンポジウム』[[小学館]], 1981 *『日本の地名』[[講談社]] 1982 *『地名と日本人：シンポジウム柳田学の継承と展開』[[講談社]] 1983 *『南島のフォークロア』共同討議 [[青土社]] 1984 *『風土学ことはじめ』雄山閣出版 1984 *『日本の神々』(全13巻) [[白水社]], 1984-1988 **新版 2000年 *『東と西：二つの日本』光村図書出版 1984 *『沖縄・奄美と日本』同成社 1986 *『日本民俗文化資料集成』(全24巻) [[三一書房]] 1988-1998 *『地名の古代史 九州篇』[[金達寿]]共著、[[河出書房新社]] 1988 *『地名の古代史 近畿篇』金達寿共著、[[河出書房新社]] 1991 **新版 2012年 *『[[南方熊楠]]、その他』思潮社 1991 *『南島の文学・民俗・歴史』山下欣一共編、三一書房 1992 *『海と列島文化6 琉球弧の世界』小学館 1992 *『[[稲生物怪録]]絵巻 江戸妖怪図録』[[小学館]] 1994 *『[[加藤清正]]：築城と治水』[[冨山房インターナショナル]] 2006 *『日琉交易の黎明：ヤマトからの衝撃』森話社 2008 *『父を語る：柳田国男と南方熊楠』冨山房インターナショナル 2010 *『海の熊野』三石学共編、森話社 2011 *『地名は警告する：日本の災害と地名』冨山房インターナショナル 2013 {{colend}}
=== 歌集 === {{colbegin|2}} *『青水沫 谷川健一歌集』三一書房 1994 *『海境 谷川健一歌集』ながらみ書房 1998 *『谷川健一全歌集』春風社 2007 *『露草の青：歌の小径』冨山房インターナショナル 2013<ref>歌論・歌集。</ref> {{colend|2}}
==主宰誌== *『青』：1999年創刊。[[根釧台地|根釧原野]]から[[八重山]]の礁湖にいたる日本列島を縦断する文化交流誌。2005年に第7号を刊行して廃刊。 *『花礁』(はなぜ)：2001年創刊。南九州と[[南西諸島]]をフィールドとする同人誌。
== 谷川健一に関する書籍 == *『「青」の民俗学：谷川健一の世界』[[岡谷公二]]・[[山下欣一]]編、三一書房 1997 *『魂の民俗学：谷川健一の思想』大江修編、[[冨山房インターナショナル]] 2006 *『谷川健一：越境する民俗学の巨人 追悼総特集』 [[河出書房新社]]([[KAWADE道の手帖]]) 2014 *『谷川健一の世界：魂の民俗学が遺したもの』大江修編、冨山房インターナショナル 2016 *『地名と風土』(人間と大地をむすぶ情報誌 15) 日本地名研究所編・出版 2022<ref>生誕百年記念。</ref> #特集1 谷川健一論の現在 #特集2 谷川健一を語る *『谷川健一と谷川雁：精神の空洞化に抗して』[[前田速夫]]編、冨山房インターナショナル 2022

[[De:Tanigawa Ken'ichi‎]] '''Tanigawa Ken'ichi''' (Japanese: 谷川 健一; born July 28, 1921, in Minamata; died August 24, 2013) was one of Japan's most prominent cultural anthropologists, particularly known for his work on historical toponymy (the study of place names).
==Biography==
After attending middle[[Category:middle]] school in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016

<!--Ken'ichi Tanigawa-->

[[De:Tanigawa Ken'ichi‎]] '''Tanigawa Ken'ichi''' (Japanese: 谷川 健一; born July 28, 1921, in Minamata; died August 24, 2013) was one of Japan's most prominent cultural anthropologists, particularly known for his work on historical toponymy (the study of place names).
==Biography==
After attending middleschool in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016
==References== <references />

[[De:Tanigawa Ken'ichi‎]] '''Tanigawa Ken'ichi''' (Japanese: 谷川 健一; born July 28, 1921, in Minamata; died August 24, 2013) was one of Japan's most prominent cultural anthropologists, particularly known for his work on historical toponymy (the study of place names).
==Biography==
After attending middleschool in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016

[[De:Tanigawa Ken'ichi‎]] '''Tanigawa Ken'ichi''' (Japanese: 谷川 健一; born July 28, 1921, in Minamata; died August 24, 2013) was one of Japan's most prominent cultural anthropologists, particularly known for his work on historical toponymy (the study of place names).
==Biography==
After attending middleschool in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016

In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016

[[De:Tanigawa Ken'ichi‎]] '''Tanigawa Ken'ichi''' (Japanese: 谷川 健一; born July 28, 1921, in Minamata; died August 24, 2013) was one of Japan's most prominent cultural anthropologists, particularly known for his work on historical toponymy (the study of place names).
==Biography==
After attending middleschool in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. [[De:Tanigawa Ken'ichi‎]] '''Tanigawa Ken'ichi''' (Japanese: 谷川 健一; born July 28, 1921, in Minamata; died August 24, 2013) was one of Japan's most prominent cultural anthropologists, particularly known for his work on historical toponymy (the study of place names).
==Biography==
After attending middleschool in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016

Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016

revision.text:
[[De:Tanigawa Ken'ichi‎]] '''Tanigawa Ken'ichi''' (Japanese: 谷川 健一; born July 28, 1921, in Minamata; died August 24, 2013) was one of Japan's most prominent cultural anthropologists, particularly known for his work on historical toponymy (the study of place names).
==Biography==
After attending middleschool in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016
==References== <references />

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:21世紀日本の民俗学者]] into [[Category:21st century Japanese folklorists]] via bot
revision.text:
[[De:Tanigawa Ken'ichi‎]] '''Tanigawa Ken'ichi''' (Japanese: 谷川 健一; born July 28, 1921, in Minamata; died August 24, 2013) was one of Japan's most prominent cultural anthropologists, particularly known for his work on historical toponymy (the study of place names).
==Biography==
After attending middleschool in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016
==References== <references />

{{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1921|7|28}}|death_date={{death date|2013|8|24}}|birth_place={{ill|Minamata|qid=Q386110}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|novelist|qid=Q6625963}}; {{ill|university teacher|qid=Q1622272}}; {{ill|waka poet|qid=Q11122954}}|relatives={{ill|Gan Tanigawa|qid=Q11632908}}|awards={{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|qid=Q2545449}}|native_name=谷川健一|given_name={{ill|Ken'ichi|qid=Q19962974}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2392596]] Wikidata name: Ken'ichi Tanigawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2392596]] Wikidata name: Ken'ichi Tanigawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[De:Tanigawa Ken'ichi‎]] '''Tanigawa Ken'ichi''' (Japanese: 谷川 健一; born July 28, 1921, in Minamata; died August 24, 2013) was one of Japan's most prominent cultural anthropologists, particularly known for his work on historical toponymy (the study of place names).
==Biography==
After attending middleschool in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016
==References== <references />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tanigawa Ken’ichi]][[ja:谷川健一]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1921|7|28}}|death_date={{death date|2013|8|24}}|birth_place={{ill|Minamata|WD=Q386110}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|novelist|WD=Q6625963}}; {{ill|university teacher|WD=Q1622272}}; {{ill|waka poet|WD=Q11122954}}|relatives={{ill|Gan Tanigawa|WD=Q11632908}}|awards={{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|WD=Q2545449}}|native_name=谷川健一|given_name={{ill|Ken'ichi|WD=Q19962974}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2392596]] Wikidata name: Ken'ichi Tanigawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2392596]] Wikidata name: Ken'ichi Tanigawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[De:Tanigawa Ken'ichi‎]] '''Tanigawa Ken'ichi''' (Japanese: 谷川 健一; born July 28, 1921, in Minamata; died August 24, 2013) was one of Japan's most prominent cultural anthropologists, particularly known for his work on historical toponymy (the study of place names).
==Biography==
After attending middleschool in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016

==Biography==
After attending middleschool in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tanigawa Ken’ichi]][[ja:谷川健一]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tanigawa Ken’ichi]][[ja:谷川健一]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tanigawa Ken’ichi]][[ja:谷川健一]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1921|7|28}}|death_date={{death date|2013|8|24}}|birth_place={{ill|Minamata|WD=Q386110}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|novelist|WD=Q6625963}}; {{ill|university teacher|WD=Q1622272}}; {{ill|waka poet|WD=Q11122954}}|relatives={{ill|Gan Tanigawa|WD=Q11632908}}|awards={{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|WD=Q2545449}}|native_name=谷川健一|given_name={{ill|Ken'ichi|WD=Q19962974}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2392596]] Wikidata name: Ken'ichi Tanigawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2392596]] Wikidata name: Ken'ichi Tanigawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[De:Tanigawa Ken'ichi‎]] '''Tanigawa Ken'ichi''' (Japanese: 谷川 健一; born July 28, 1921, in Minamata; died August 24, 2013) was one of Japan's most prominent cultural anthropologists, particularly known for his work on historical toponymy (the study of place names).
==Biography==
After attending middleschool in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016

[[De:Tanigawa Ken'ichi‎]] '''Tanigawa Ken'ichi''' (Japanese: 谷川 健一; born July 28, 1921, in Minamata; died August 24, 2013) was one of Japan's most prominent cultural anthropologists, particularly known for his work on historical toponymy (the study of place names).
==Biography==
After attending middleschool in Kumamoto, Tanigawa studied French literature at the Imperial University of Tokyo.
Tanigawa gathered information on the lifestyle and culture of the lower social strata, traditions, rites, and legends associated with the coast as a buffer zone between sea and land, and between concepts of the afterlife and this life. His specialty was place names. Numerous publications appeared starting in 1957, initially through Heibonsha. In the 1960s, he was involved in an academic dispute with Origuchi Shinobu (折口 信夫) and Yanagita Kunio (1875–1962; 柳田 國男).
His 1973 publication "Collection of Historical Biographies of Common People" (日本庶民生活史料集成) comprises 20 volumes, and the "Compendium of Japanese Folk Customs" (1986; 日本民俗文化大系) consists of 14 volumes. In 1981, the city of Kawasaki established the "Institute for the Study of Japanese Place Names" (日本地名研究所), of which he became the director and from which he received an award in 2008. From 1987 to 1996, he taught at Kinki University in Osaka, where he also headed the university's anthropological institute.
For his research achievements, he received several academic awards, including one in 2001 for his Tanka collection. In 2007, he was honored as a "Person of Cultural Merit" (文化功労者).
==Works and Literature==
His collected minor works have been published progressively since 1980 by San'ichi in 10 volumes. Translations into German by a team from the University of Fukui include:
- Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Foreword/The Home Beyond the Sea), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2005 (first published in 1983 by Heibonsha) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Afterlife/The Birthplaces of Wakasa/The Southern Islands), in: Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, Vol. 61 (2005), 62 (2006) - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Niraikanai and the Green Islands), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2007 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Sea of Koshi/Records from Shima), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, Series I, Humanities, 64, 2009 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Urashima Legend from Tango), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2014 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (The Green Graves of Mino), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2015 - Sho Hayashi, Paulus Kaufmann, Isozaki Kotaro (translation): "About the Afterlife - Where the Soul of the Japanese Strives" (Afterword and Postscript), Memoirs of the Faculty of Education and Regional Studies, University of Fukui, 2016
